course_description:
The course examines the design and management principles and practices of sustainable visitor experiences in tourism, hospitality and event operations and destinations. It will examine settings ranging from the global to local landscapes, streetscapes and servicescapes. Topics include visitor motives, decision making and behaviour; the experience economy; designing and staging experiences; orientation and place making; interpretation and visitor learning; and cross-cultural and social interactions. This course will enhance students' employability by giving them the skills and insights to systematically critique and improve visitor experiences offered at tourist attractions.

This course will provide students with an understanding of the dynamics of visitor behaviour and experiences across a range of visitor settings. Key areas of discussion include motivation, visitor learning, interpretation, managing visitor impacts, risk management and designing effective and enjoyable visitor experiences.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

One of the assessments has been modified to better align with a workplace/ professional scenario.

course_aims: This course teaches students about theﾠprinciples and practiceﾠof designing and managing visitor experiences in a range of tourism settings. Through a variety of activities, students explore key aspects of visitor management including visitor learning, interpretation, visitor research and evaluation, orientation and way-finding, minimising negative impacts and risk reduction. Thisﾠcourse enhances students'ﾠemployability by giving them the skills and insights to systematically critiqueﾠand improveﾠvisitor experiences offered atﾠtouristﾠattractions.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Explain the dynamics of motivation and how this impacts on visitors' experiences in a range of tourism, hospitality and events settings
Apply the key principles of visitor learning, interpretation and orientation to the design of visitor experiences
Identify and manage environmental, social and cross-cultural impacts in visitor settings
Develop strategies and recommendations for the management of visitor experiences in tourism, hospitality and event operations and destinations
Critically evaluate a visitor experience using a range of evaluation tools and approaches.

assessment_summary.title: Management Consultant Briefing
assessment_summary.due_date: Week 7 - Week 8; Week 7 - Week 8; This is an in-class assessment.

assessment_details.title: Critique of Visitor Site
assessment_details.task_description: Select a suitable visitor attraction and conduct a critique of the site in terms of visitor learning experiences and visitor management. You CANNOT choose the Queensland Museum as we will be visiting this site as a field trip. You will be doing this from the perspective of ONE particular target market of your choice (eg. primary school children on excursions; families with teenage children; Australian retirees with an interest in history etc). Word Limit: 1000 words You are required to: Select a visitor site that offers some level or type of visitor learning. You will need to visit the site but can also use virtual tours and other online resources to inform your critique. Using the guidelines from the Queensland Museum field trip, write a critique of the various visitor facilities and services offered at your chosen site. Include photographs to illustrate your points, including a selfie of you at your site. PART 1: BACKGROUND Describe the site – what is it (museum, national park etc), where is it, how long has it been there etc. Provide a map or diagram of the site Identify ONE target market that you will be using as the basis of your report and briefly discuss their likely motives and interests. PART 2: CRITIQUE Drawing from relevant lecture content and using the checklist provided, note how well the site is likely to meet the needs and interests of the ONE target market you have identified. In particular, you should discuss: Entrance/welcoming area Visitor facilities Visitor comfort Orientation (both internal and external directional signs) Visitor engagement Interpretive signs Exhibit design issues Maintenance Highlight the areas and aspects that are done well and those that could be improved. Justify this with your on-site observations and photographs. A template will be provided on Blackboard for submitting this assessment. Assessment 1 (Critique of Visitor Site) and Assessment 2 (Management Consultant Briefing) are related. The critique you develop in Assessment 1 and the feedback given will be used as the basis of Assessment 2. AI Statement This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension

The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Management Consultant Briefing
assessment_details.due_date: Week 7 - Week 8 Week 7 - Week 8 This is an in-class assessment.
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 (Critique of Visitor Site) and Assessment 2 (Management Consultant Briefing) are related. The critique you have developed in Assessment 1 and the feedback given will be used as the basis of Assessment 2. As a consultant, provide the manager of a tourism operation with recommendations for enhancing the visitor experience for a target market. The recommendations are based on your first assessment (Site Critique Task). You will be doing this from the perspective of ONE target market of your choice (e.g. primary school children on field trips; families with teenage children; international tourists on a tour etc). Consultant Briefing The recommendations are to be presented as a consultant in a 10 min professional (oral) conversation with the tourism manager. Your course coordinator or tutor will role play the tourism manager. Format The meeting will consist of two parts: Part 1 - Presenting 2–3 priority recommendations Your recommendations are directly related to the Site Critique task you submitted earlier and should: • Provide suggestions for improvements to the identified weaknesses. You can also provide suggestions for how the site can further improve their strengths. • Be clearly linked to the needs and preferences of the ONE target market you have selected. • Be innovative, strategic, feasible, realistic and measurable. Clearly state what is recommended and how to achieve this. For example, it is insufficient to say there are no multi-lingual signs so the recommendation is to install multi-lingual signs. Rather, you should be stating WHY multilingual signs would be beneficial, WHERE they should be placed and WHAT specific languages should be considered. Part 2 - Conversation with your manager You will be asked questions about your recommendations. Specifically, you will be asked about: • how you applied what you learned about the key principles of the visitor experience in the course so far to the design of your recommendations. • your recommendations with regards to your research in the Site Critique and learning in the first part of the course. Purpose The task is an opportunity to: • Propose evidence based, feasible recommendations that improve visitor experience, accessibility, sustainability, cultural safety, and commercial outcomes. • Showcase your knowledge and learning situated in a scenario aligned to a workplace/professional scenario. • Develop your skills to communicate professionally with a manager in the tourism sector. No printed materials or notes are to be used for this briefing. Please Note: The presentation will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy. AI-Statement This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Facility Design Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: FACILITY DESIGN PROPOSAL Word Count: Approx. 3,500 This is an individual assignment that requires you to design an ORIGINAL tourism/event or hospitality facility. Choose from one of the following categories: a theme park, zoo, or cultural attraction a museum, art gallery or historic building a resort, health spa, hotel/casino complex (NOT A GYM) an entertainment complex PART 1: Introduction Overview of proposal an overview of the design concept. brief background information about the location of your facility and its surroundings. explanation of facility name. Target Market, motives and interests In this section you should identify ONE market segment that you feel will be particularly attracted to the facility that you are proposing: Who are they, what are they interested in? Why is your facility likely to attract this market? Why do you think your proposal will be successful? Here you should briefly justify with literature dealing with the type of facility that you are proposing. The literature can include trade journals, media reports and non-academic sources if relevant. PART 2: Design elements Theme What are the underlying concept or ideas linking the elements together? What is the key thing you want visitors to think, feel or do as a result of visiting your facility? Layout and Orientation Design a map or site plan of the facility. Describe and justify your layout. Describe the different ‘areas’ and explain how the site has been designed to facilitate visitor use, flows and movement. If relevant, explain how you would manage queues, crowding and capacity issues. Design elements Describe how you intend to use ambient features (lighting, music, colour, décor) to support your theme and enhance your visitors’ experiences. Describe the activities and experiences that will be offered. Explain how these support your theme. Provide photos and diagrams to illustrate your design ideas. Managing Impacts Describe how you will manage the on-site visitor experience: Identify one environmental impact and one socio-cultural impact that your facility could create. These can be both positive, both negative, or one of each. Any combination is permissible, but only identify two in total . Explain what visitor management strategies you would use to minimise/maximise the two identified impacts. REFERENCE LIST APPENDICES (OPTIONAL) Insert illustrations into the text of your assignment. The appendices is for diagrams, graphs or tables you would like to refer to as supporting information. Appendices and references are not included in the word limit. AI Statement This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Introduction: What is Visitor Management? Course Introduction, Overview and Orientation Activities: This first week we will get to know each other and ensure we have a shared understanding of the key terms used in this course. We will also explore the course profile so you know where to look for key information throughout the semester. Learning outcomes: L01

Understanding your visitors: Motives and target markets It is important to understand our visitors, in particular their motives for the visitor experiences they seek. We will explore different target markets and their motivations. This sets the scene for a more indepth discussion about the importance of visitor interpretation next week. Activities will relate to understanding different target markets, (including undertaking a personality quiz) and tourist motivations. Learning outcomes: L01

Interpretation: Principles and practices of interpretation This week we will delve into the importance of understanding visitor interpretation. What is it and how can tourism providers do it well? We will build interpretation skills by incorporating a storytelling exercise. Learning outcomes: L02, L04

Designing Interpretive Signs: Principles & Pitfalls Continuing with our understanding of visitor interpretation, this week we will critically evaluate the suitability and effectiveness of signs in different TH&E contexts. To do this, we will get out of the classroom and explore UQ campus museums and various visitor spaces around the campus. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05

Visitor Studies: Field trip to Queensland Museum Evaluating visitor experiences through field work. This will be a practical and applied class excursion to the QLD Museum at Southbank. All the details will be provided in class. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Orientation, Maps & Way-finding; Visitor Learning Due to the public holiday you will be provided with online resources to guide you in identifying elements of effective maps and designing experiences for visitors with different learning styles. Public Holiday - Friday, 3rd April 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about online materials and recommended exercises. Learning outcomes: L02, L05

Interactive Oral Assessments Assessment task 2: Management Consultant Briefing. In-class. Students will self-select a time slot to deliver this assessment task. Instructions will be provided on Blackboard. An online self-paced learning module will be available on blackboard to get started on Assessment Task 3 Facility Design Proposal. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05

Interactive Oral Assessments (Continued) Assessment task 2: Management Consultant Briefing. In-class. Students will self-select a time slot to deliver this assessment task. Instructions will be provided on Blackboard. An online self-paced learning module will be available on blackboard to get started on Assessment Task 3 Facility Design Proposal. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05

Principles of Design: Colour, soundscapes, textual factors & spatial design This week we investigate the fascinating principles of site and experience design and how use of colours and sounds can make or break a visitor experience. Activities will be related to understanding the impact of colour, soundscapes, textual factors and spatial design on the visitor experience. Learning outcomes: L02, L04

Designing Visitor Experiences It sounds easy but designing quality visitor experiences takes skill and creativity. This week's activities are related to creating multi-sensory experiences and themed tourist spaces and attractions. This will help generate some great ideas for the final assignment. Learning outcomes: L02, L04

Managing crowds, queues & risks Nobody really likes waiting in line for anything! As providers of effective visitor experiences we need to develop strategies for managing large numbers of visitors at a tourist attraction. Activities will relate to this important aspect. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Managing Visitor Impacts This week we examine major visitor impacts that can ruin great experiences. We will discuss effective approaches to managing visitors’ impacts on communities and the built and natural environment. This content will assist with the final assignment. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Future Trends in Visitor Management: Future trends and challenges; the role of technology This is the final week and we will wrap up with a discussion on visitor management challenges of the future. We might even finish with a super interactive and engaging Digital Escape Box Activity! Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05
